articles.headline: Iran Retaliates Across Middle East as US-Israel Strikes Kill Hundreds
articles.subheadline: Conflict engulfs Gulf states, with attacks on Qatar, UAE, Saudi Arabia, and a British base in Cyprus as oil prices surge
articles.body:
A rapidly escalating military conflict between the United States, Israel, and Iran has spread across the Middle East and into the Gulf, with Iranian retaliatory strikes hitting targets in at least seven countries and an Iranian drone striking a British Royal Air Force base in Cyprus overnight Sunday.

The crisis began over the weekend when joint US-Israeli strikes targeted Iran's political and military infrastructure, killing Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei and other senior figures in Tehran. Satellite imagery confirmed significant damage to the Leadership House compound, and BBC Verify has identified strikes across at least 13 locations in the capital and 12 other Iranian cities. The Iranian Red Crescent reported at least 555 people killed, including 153 at a girls' school in the southern city of Minab struck by three missiles — a figure that has not been independently verified. Four US service personnel were killed and five seriously injured during the assault. President Donald Trump said US forces had sunk 10 Iranian naval vessels and "largely destroyed" Iran's naval headquarters, warning that operations would continue with "full force" until Washington's objectives are met.

Iran's retaliation has been wide-ranging. Three people were killed in the United Arab Emirates, one in Kuwait, and four wounded in Bahrain, where a drone strike caused a major fire at a US naval installation on Sunday evening. In Qatar, authorities said they shot down two Iranian aircraft, seven missiles, and five drones after strikes targeted a major gas facility. Saudi Arabia's Ras Tanura refinery — operated by state oil giant Aramco — was hit by drones, though officials said the resulting fire was brought under control Monday. Jordan intercepted ballistic missiles over its territory, and Oman reported drone strikes on its Duqm commercial port. In a particularly alarming incident, Kuwait's military downed three US fighter jets Monday in what US Central Command called "an apparent friendly fire incident," though all six crew members ejected safely.

The conflict's reach extended to Europe when an Iranian drone crashed into RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus at midnight Sunday, prompting London to evacuate family members from the base. Two additional drones targeting the installation were intercepted Monday. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer told Parliament that Trump had expressed disagreement with the UK's decision not to participate in the initial strikes on Iran.

Global energy markets reacted sharply. European natural gas prices surged more than 30 percent after Qatar Energy — the world's largest producer of liquefied natural gas, supplying roughly a fifth of global LNG — halted production. Oil prices also jumped amid reports of attacks on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global energy supplies. Meanwhile, the fighting in Lebanon continued unabated, with Israel striking Hezbollah targets in Beirut's southern suburbs and southern Lebanon after the group fired rockets at the Israeli city of Haifa. At least 31 people were killed in Lebanon on Monday, and an Iranian missile killed nine people sheltering in an Israeli city the previous day. Trump's stated objectives for the campaign remain unclear, with no public articulation of what a post-war Iran might look like.

articles.headline: Unified Analytical Framework Derived for Photoacoustic Wave Sources
articles.subheadline: Researchers present general closed-form solutions for spherically symmetric photoacoustic signals, with open-source simulation code
articles.body:
A team of researchers has derived a comprehensive analytical framework that unifies the mathematical description of acoustic pressure waves generated by spherically symmetric photoacoustic sources — a result that could streamline the design and analysis of photoacoustic imaging systems used in biomedical diagnostics and materials science.

The work, posted to the arXiv preprint server by Shuang Li, Yibing Wang, Yu Zhang, and Changhui Li, starts from the fundamental photoacoustic wave equation and arrives at a single general solution valid for any spherically symmetric initial pressure distribution. From that master expression, the authors extract specific closed-form results for the most commonly encountered source profiles: uniform spherical distributions, Gaussian distributions, exponential distributions, and power-law distributions. Far-field approximations, which simplify calculations when the detector sits far from the source, are also provided.

Photoacoustic imaging works by illuminating tissue or other materials with short laser pulses. The absorbed light causes rapid thermoelastic expansion, launching ultrasonic pressure waves that are detected and reconstructed into images. Accurate forward models — mathematical descriptions of how a given source distribution produces a measurable acoustic signal — are essential for both system calibration and image reconstruction algorithms. Until now, researchers often relied on numerical solvers or case-by-case derivations for different source geometries, an approach that can be computationally expensive and difficult to generalize.

By consolidating these cases under a single analytical umbrella, the new framework offers what the authors call "ultrafast forward simulation." They have released their implementation as open-source code on GitHub, lowering the barrier for other groups to adopt the model in their own imaging pipelines. The availability of exact expressions also makes it easier to benchmark numerical codes and to build intuition about how source geometry shapes the detected waveform.

The paper, classified under optics and computer vision on arXiv, reflects a growing trend of bridging analytical physics with computational imaging. While the immediate applications lie in photoacoustic microscopy and tomography — techniques increasingly used for vascular imaging, tumor detection, and functional brain mapping — the mathematical machinery is general enough to find use wherever spherically symmetric acoustic sources arise, from non-destructive testing to underwater acoustics.

articles.headline: Popular Anti-Aging Compound Spermidine May Also Drive Cancer Growth
articles.subheadline: Japanese researchers uncover how polyamines activate different molecular pathways in healthy cells versus tumors
articles.body:
A class of molecules widely promoted as longevity supplements may carry a significant hidden risk: fueling cancer cell proliferation through a mechanism that closely mirrors — but critically diverges from — the same pathway responsible for their anti-aging benefits.

Researchers at Tokyo University of Science, led by Associate Professor Kyohei Higashi, have identified how polyamines, particularly spermidine, activate two nearly identical proteins to produce starkly opposite biological outcomes. In healthy tissue, polyamines stimulate a protein called eIF5A1 that promotes autophagy — the cellular housekeeping process that clears damaged components and supports healthy aging. But in cancerous tissue, the same molecules instead boost production of a closely related protein, eIF5A2, which shares 84% of eIF5A1's amino acid sequence yet drives rapid tumor growth by enhancing aerobic glycolysis, the metabolic shortcut cancer cells use to generate energy. The findings were published in the Journal of Biological Chemistry.

The team's approach was methodical. Working with human cancer cell lines, the researchers first depleted polyamine levels using a drug, then reintroduced spermidine to directly measure its effects. Using high-resolution proteomic analysis spanning more than 6,700 proteins, they found that polyamines in cancer cells primarily amplified glycolysis rather than mitochondrial respiration — the energy pathway more closely associated with healthy aging. Polyamines also elevated levels of eIF5A2 and five ribosomal proteins linked to cancer severity, including RPS 27A, RPL36AL, and RPL22L1.

Crucially, the researchers uncovered the mechanism by which polyamines increase eIF5A2 production. Under normal conditions, a small regulatory RNA molecule called miR-6514-5p acts as a brake on eIF5A2 synthesis. Polyamines disrupt this molecular checkpoint, allowing eIF5A2 levels to rise unchecked. "In normal tissues, eIF5A1, activated by polyamines, activates mitochondria via autophagy, whereas in cancer tissues, eIF5A2, whose synthesis is promoted by polyamines, controls gene expression at the translational level to facilitate the proliferation of cancer cells," Higashi explained.

The findings carry immediate implications for both cancer therapeutics and the booming supplements market, where spermidine products are marketed as geroprotectors. The research suggests that targeting eIF5A2 specifically could slow tumor growth without disrupting the beneficial effects mediated by eIF5A1 — a distinction that could inform a new class of selective cancer treatments. For consumers, the results underscore that biological context matters profoundly: molecules that support cellular health in normal tissue may behave very differently in tissue that is cancerous or precancerous. The study was supported by the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science, the Hamaguchi Foundation for the Advancement of Biochemistry, and the Cancer Research Institute at Kanazawa University.

articles.headline: Paramount+ and HBO Max to Merge Into Single Streaming Platform
articles.subheadline: CEO David Ellison unveils plans for a 200-million-subscriber service after Paramount's $110 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery
articles.body:
Paramount Skydance CEO David Ellison announced Monday that the company will combine Paramount+ and HBO Max into a single streaming service, following its agreement to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery in a deal valued at approximately $110 billion. The merger would create one of the largest content libraries in entertainment history, uniting franchises spanning Harry Potter, Game of Thrones, Star Trek, Top Gun, Yellowstone, and Looney Tunes under a single digital roof.

The announcement came after Netflix unexpectedly withdrew from its own bid to acquire WBD, opening the door for Paramount Skydance to step in. On an investor call, Ellison pledged to preserve HBO's distinct creative identity — "Our viewpoint is HBO should stay HBO" — while committing to an ambitious theatrical pipeline of at least 30 films per year, split evenly between the two legacy studios. The combined streaming platform is projected to boast more than 200 million subscribers, positioning it as a formidable rival to Netflix and the Disney+-Hulu combination.

The deal accelerates a sweeping wave of consolidation reshaping Hollywood's streaming landscape. But the sheer scale of the transaction has already drawn regulatory attention. The U.S. Department of Justice is expected to scrutinize the merger over media concentration concerns, and California Attorney General Rob Bonta said last week that his office would conduct a rigorous review of the acquisition. Industry analysts also anticipate significant workforce reductions as overlapping operations are streamlined, adding to anxieties already running high across the entertainment sector.

Beyond antitrust and labor questions, the merger raises editorial independence concerns. Critics have pointed to the Ellison family's political ties to former President Donald Trump and the potential implications for newsrooms at CBS and CNN, both of which would fall under the combined company's umbrella. Ellison sought to get ahead of such criticism, describing the transaction as "pro-competition, pro-consumer, and pro-creative community" and arguing it would expand consumer choice while strengthening Hollywood's global production ecosystem.

If approved, the deal would represent one of the largest media mergers in history, fundamentally redrawing the competitive map of an industry still searching for sustainable economics in the streaming era. Regulatory proceedings are expected to stretch well into the coming months.

articles.headline: Oil Prices Surge as Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to a Halt
articles.subheadline: Middle East conflict disrupts the world's most critical oil chokepoint, with Saudi refinery operations also impacted
articles.body:
Oil prices spiked sharply on Saturday as escalating conflict in the Middle East brought shipping through the Strait of Hormuz to a near standstill, threatening to choke off roughly one-fifth of the world's daily petroleum supply and sending shockwaves through global energy markets.

The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman through which approximately 20 million barrels of oil pass each day, has long been considered the single most critical chokepoint in global energy infrastructure. The near-total halt in vessel traffic marks the most severe disruption to the strait in decades, surpassing tensions during past confrontations in the Persian Gulf. Compounding the crisis, a major Saudi Arabian refinery has also been impacted by the conflict, further tightening supply conditions in a market already on edge.

The dual shock — restricted crude transit and reduced refining capacity — represents a scenario energy analysts have long warned about but few expected to materialize simultaneously. Saudi Arabia, the world's largest crude exporter, relies heavily on the Hormuz corridor to move oil to customers across Asia and beyond. Any sustained closure would force producers in Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Iraq, and Qatar to seek alternative, far more costly and limited export routes, if they exist at all.

Global consumers and governments now face the prospect of rapidly tightening supply at a moment when strategic petroleum reserves in major economies remain below historical norms following drawdowns in recent years. The International Energy Agency has previously estimated that a full Hormuz closure lasting more than a few days could trigger price increases of historic proportions, potentially dwarfing the spikes seen during the 1973 oil embargo or the 2022 disruptions following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

Market participants will be closely watching diplomatic channels and military developments in the coming hours for any signals of de-escalation. In the absence of a swift resolution, energy traders and policy officials worldwide are bracing for sustained volatility and the possibility that oil prices could climb significantly further, with cascading effects on inflation, transportation costs, and economic growth across both developed and emerging economies.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Rallies Past $68,000 as Markets Absorb Iran Conflict
articles.subheadline: Crypto and equities recover sharply from overnight lows as strong U.S. manufacturing data and short-covering fuel Monday's rebound
articles.body:
Bitcoin surged above $68,600 on Monday, climbing 2.3% in 24 hours as financial markets proved far more resilient than feared in the wake of U.S. military strikes against Iran over the weekend. The cryptocurrency's advance came alongside a dramatic recovery in U.S. equities, with the Nasdaq falling just 0.1% after overnight futures had pointed to a plunge exceeding 2%.

The muted reaction in risk assets stood in contrast to traditional safe havens, which posted robust gains. Gold rose 2%, crude oil jumped 7% on supply disruption fears, and the U.S. dollar index notched one of its strongest sessions in weeks with a 1% advance. Crypto-related equities outperformed the broader market, with stablecoin issuer Circle surging 12%, Strategy — the Michael Saylor-led bitcoin treasury company — gaining 6%, and Galaxy Digital climbing 4.7%.

Analysts cautioned, however, that bitcoin's rally may lack durable foundations. According to market observers cited by CoinDesk, Monday's move was driven primarily by short-covering and leveraged positioning rather than fresh spot buying. Rising open interest and large liquidation clusters around $65,000 and above $70,000 suggest the advance could prove fragile without sustained demand from spot buyers and exchange-traded fund inflows.

The macro backdrop added complexity to the outlook. The ISM manufacturing purchasing managers' index came in at 52.4 for February, marking consecutive months of expansion above 50 for the first time since late 2022. Friday's Chicago Business Barometer had already surprised to the upside at 57.7, reflecting the strongest pace of U.S. activity growth since May 2022. Combined with hotter-than-expected producer price data last week and rising energy costs tied to Middle East tensions, the data effectively eliminated any prospect of a Federal Reserve rate cut at its March 18 meeting.

Under normal circumstances, the evaporation of rate-cut expectations would weigh on speculative assets like cryptocurrencies. But market participants appeared to have already adjusted their positioning for tighter-for-longer monetary policy, limiting the impact. Meanwhile, the broader crypto ecosystem showed signs of heightened geopolitical activity: blockchain analytics firm Elliptic reported that Iranian crypto outflows surged 700% within minutes of the U.S.-Israeli airstrikes, and prediction market Polymarket attracted record volumes as bets on the Iran conflict topped $529 million. Ether, Solana, and XRP all posted gains between 1% and 2% on the session.

articles.headline: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Surge as Iran War Fallout Shuts Qatar LNG Plant
articles.subheadline: Qatar's closure of the world's largest LNG facility sends shockwaves through global energy markets
articles.body:
U.S. natural gas futures climbed sharply on Monday as global energy prices surged following Qatar's decision to shut down production at the world's largest liquefied natural gas plant, a dramatic escalation in the economic fallout from the ongoing war in Iran.

The closure of Qatar's flagship LNG facility — a cornerstone of global gas supply — sent immediate ripples through international energy markets, lifting prices across crude oil and natural gas benchmarks worldwide. U.S. futures rose in sympathy with the global rally, reflecting the deeply interconnected nature of energy commodities even as the United States remains a major gas producer in its own right.

Qatar is one of the world's top LNG exporters, and the shuttering of its largest production complex removes a significant volume of supply from a market already navigating heightened geopolitical uncertainty. The conflict in Iran, which shares maritime borders with Qatar across the Persian Gulf, has raised persistent concerns about the security of energy infrastructure and shipping routes in the region. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil and a substantial share of global LNG cargoes transit, sits at the center of these anxieties.

The price spike underscores the fragility of global energy supply chains, particularly for natural gas, which has become an increasingly critical fuel for power generation and industrial use worldwide. European and Asian spot LNG prices, already elevated heading into March, face further upside pressure as buyers scramble to secure alternative cargoes. For U.S. producers and exporters, the disruption could translate into stronger demand for American LNG shipments, potentially tightening domestic supply balances.

Crude oil prices also advanced alongside natural gas, reflecting broader market fears that the Iran conflict could widen further and disrupt additional energy production or transit routes in the Middle East. Analysts warned that continued instability in the region could sustain elevated energy prices well beyond the near term, with implications for inflation, industrial costs, and energy policy decisions in importing nations across Europe and Asia.

articles.headline: Buterin Proposes Anti-Censorship Measures to Fix Ethereum's Builder Centralization
articles.subheadline: Ethereum co-founder outlines encrypted transactions, random inclusion committees, and distributed block building
articles.body:
Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin has published a sweeping blueprint to address what he calls one of the network's most pressing but least visible vulnerabilities: the growing concentration of power among the small number of entities that assemble transaction blocks.

In a blog post published Monday, Buterin outlined several proposals designed to prevent block builders — the actors who order and package transactions before they are committed to the blockchain — from gaining outsized influence over which transactions get processed. The concern is not theoretical. As Ethereum prepares its forthcoming "Glamsterdam" upgrade, which will formally separate the roles of block proposers and block builders, Buterin argued that merely creating a competitive marketplace for builders is insufficient. A handful of dominant builders could still censor transactions or extract disproportionate profits from ordinary users.

Among the most concrete proposals is FOCIL, a mechanism that would randomly select a small committee of participants to each nominate transactions that must be included in the next block. If those transactions are absent, the block would be rejected by the network. The design functions as an anti-censorship backstop: even if a single hostile builder controlled the entire market, it could not permanently exclude specific users or transactions. Buterin also took aim at so-called "toxic MEV" — maximal extractable value strategies in which sophisticated traders exploit their ability to see pending transactions, front-running or "sandwiching" ordinary users' trades for profit. One proposed countermeasure involves encrypting transactions until they are finalized, rendering them invisible to opportunistic actors. He further suggested that anonymized transaction routing at the network layer could prevent intermediaries from surveilling transactions before they reach a builder at all.

Looking further ahead, Buterin sketched a vision of more distributed block construction, arguing that much of Ethereum's activity does not require the tight global ordering currently imposed by single-block assembly. Relaxing that constraint could reduce central chokepoints and open the door to architectures where no single entity wields decisive power over transaction inclusion.

The proposals arrive at a pivotal moment for Ethereum, which was trading near $2,046 on Monday, up roughly 6.8% on the day. As the network scales through layer-2 rollups and protocol upgrades, Buterin's post signals a strategic shift in where he sees decentralization risks migrating — away from validators and toward the less visible infrastructure layer that determines what actually makes it on-chain. Whether the community coalesces around these ideas will likely shape Ethereum's governance debates for years to come.

articles.headline: Treasuries Tumble as Oil Spike Revives Inflation Concerns
articles.subheadline: A 6% surge in crude prices tied to Iran conflict disrupts rate-cut expectations and rattles bond markets
articles.body:
U.S. Treasury prices fell sharply on Saturday as a dramatic jump in oil prices — driven by escalating conflict involving Iran — reignited fears of persistent inflation and pushed traders to reassess the likelihood of near-term interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve.

Crude oil surged approximately 6% after the Iran war raised the specter of disruptions to critical shipping lanes, a scenario that could constrain global energy supplies and feed directly into consumer prices. The move sent bond yields climbing as investors priced in a higher-for-longer rate environment, eroding expectations that the Fed would ease monetary policy in the months ahead. The U.S. dollar strengthened in tandem, benefiting from safe-haven flows and widening interest rate differentials.

Equity markets proved more resilient than initially expected. After opening lower, major stock indices clawed back their losses over the course of the session, suggesting that investors viewed the geopolitical disruption as more of an inflation story than a growth-destruction event — at least for now. The recovery in equities may also reflect confidence that U.S. corporations, particularly in the energy sector, could benefit from elevated commodity prices.

The market reaction underscores the fragile calculus facing policymakers at the Federal Reserve. With inflation expectations already proving stubborn in recent months, a sustained oil price shock could further complicate the central bank's path toward rate reductions. Fed funds futures shifted to reflect diminished odds of a cut at upcoming meetings, a meaningful repricing that could weigh on rate-sensitive sectors including housing and technology.

Analysts cautioned that the situation remains fluid. The extent of any shipping lane disruptions, the trajectory of the military conflict, and the response from major oil-producing nations will all determine whether the crude rally has staying power or fades as geopolitical risk premiums often do. For now, however, bond traders are sending a clear signal: the inflation fight may not be over.

articles.headline: Gasoline Futures Spike After US-Israel Strikes on Iran
articles.subheadline: Rising fuel prices threaten to undermine a central pillar of Trump's energy agenda as geopolitical tensions escalate
articles.body:
Gasoline and diesel futures jumped sharply on the first trading day following US and Israeli military strikes on Iran, introducing a significant new vulnerability for President Donald Trump's energy platform, which has relied heavily on promises of affordable American fuel.

The price surge, triggered by the escalation of hostilities against one of the world's major oil-producing nations, risks eroding what had been one of the administration's most politically advantageous talking points. Trump has repeatedly touted his "drill, baby, drill" agenda and pledged to bring down energy costs for American consumers — a message that resonated strongly during his campaign and early months in office. Rising prices at the pump could complicate that narrative at a time when the administration is already navigating trade tensions and tariff-related economic uncertainty.

Iran, an OPEC member that produces roughly 3 million barrels of oil per day, sits along the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately one-fifth of the world's petroleum supply transits daily. Any sustained disruption to Iranian output or regional shipping lanes could send crude prices significantly higher, with downstream effects on gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and petrochemical feedstocks. Futures markets moved swiftly to price in these risks as traders returned to their desks following the military action.

The political calculus around energy prices is particularly sensitive for the Trump White House. Gasoline prices function as one of the most visible economic indicators for ordinary Americans, displayed on signs at every street corner and felt directly in household budgets. Previous administrations of both parties have suffered politically when pump prices climbed, regardless of the underlying cause. The Biden administration faced sustained criticism over elevated fuel costs in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine — a dynamic Trump exploited effectively on the campaign trail.

Analysts will be watching closely in the coming days to assess whether the price spike proves transient or marks the beginning of a more sustained upward trend. The trajectory will depend largely on whether the conflict with Iran escalates further, whether Iranian oil exports face additional disruption, and how global energy markets absorb the shock. For the administration, the challenge will be reconciling an aggressive foreign policy posture in the Middle East with its domestic promise of cheap, abundant American energy.

articles.headline: Oil Surges Most in Four Years as Strait of Hormuz Tanker Traffic Halts
articles.subheadline: Benchmark diesel futures jump 23% and European natural gas prices soar as Iran conflict chokes global energy flows
articles.body:
Oil prices recorded their sharpest single-session rally in four years on March 2 as the escalating war involving Iran brought tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — the world's most critical energy chokepoint — to a virtual standstill, triggering a cascading shock across global commodity markets.

Benchmark diesel futures surged 23%, while crude oil and European natural gas prices both spiked dramatically as traders scrambled to price in the sudden disruption to a waterway that in normal times handles roughly one-fifth of the world's daily petroleum consumption. Ellen Wald, president of Transversal Consulting, told Bloomberg that the conflict's direct impact on Hormuz transit routes has fundamentally altered the near-term supply picture, leaving refiners and importers facing an acute shortage of seaborne crude and fuel cargoes.

The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow passage between Iran and Oman at the mouth of the Persian Gulf, serves as the sole maritime export route for much of the oil produced by Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Qatar. Its effective closure represents a scenario long feared by energy strategists but rarely realized at this scale. With tanker operators unwilling to risk passage through an active conflict zone, millions of barrels per day of supply are now stranded, and importing nations in Asia and Europe face the prospect of drawing down strategic reserves if the disruption persists.

The energy price shock is already reverberating through downstream markets. Gasoline futures climbed sharply, raising the specter of rapid increases at the pump for consumers in the United States and Europe. Natural gas markets, already tight heading into the final weeks of the Northern Hemisphere heating season, face additional strain as liquefied natural gas shipments from Qatar — the world's largest LNG exporter — depend on the same imperiled corridor.

Analysts warned that the duration of the disruption will determine whether the spike remains a short-lived event or evolves into a sustained supply crisis. Should hostilities intensify or spread to physical attacks on energy infrastructure, prices could climb further still, with knock-on effects for inflation, central bank policy, and global economic growth. For now, markets are bracing for volatility of a magnitude not seen since the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic upended energy demand in 2020.

articles.headline: Qatar Shoots Down Two Iranian Fighter Jets in Conflict First
articles.subheadline: The downing of two Su-24 bombers marks the first known destruction of crewed Iranian aircraft in combat since hostilities began
articles.body:
Qatar's air force has shot down two Iranian Su-24 fighter-bombers, marking the first confirmed destruction of crewed Iranian military aircraft in combat since fighting erupted between Iran and a Gulf coalition last week, according to an announcement from Qatar's Ministry of Defense on Sunday.

The two Sukhoi Su-24 jets — Soviet-era variable-sweep wing bombers that have long formed a backbone of Iran's aging air fleet — were intercepted and destroyed in what represents a significant escalation in the aerial dimensions of the conflict. While many of Iran's combat aircraft were reportedly destroyed on the ground during the opening wave of coalition airstrikes designed to degrade Tehran's defensive and offensive capabilities, the downing of actively flying, crewed warplanes introduces a new and potentially volatile chapter in the hostilities.

The engagement underscores the growing intensity of a conflict that has rapidly drawn in multiple Gulf states and rattled global energy markets. The war, which began last week, has centered on longstanding tensions in the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply transits daily. Any sustained disruption to traffic through the strait could send shockwaves through global crude markets, which were already pricing in elevated risk premiums in the days following the outbreak of hostilities.

Iran's air force has been widely assessed by defense analysts as technologically outmatched by Gulf adversaries, many of whom operate advanced Western-supplied aircraft. The Su-24, a twin-seat, twin-engine attack aircraft first introduced in the 1970s, has seen service in multiple conflicts but is considered increasingly obsolete against modern air defense systems. The early coalition strategy of targeting Iranian air assets on the ground appears to have been aimed at neutralizing this fleet before it could be deployed, though Sunday's shootdown suggests some aircraft remain operational.

The broader conflict continues to raise alarm over Iran's nuclear program and the potential for further escalation across the Middle East. No immediate response from Tehran regarding the loss of the two jets has been reported. International observers and energy analysts are closely monitoring the situation, with oil futures expected to face renewed upward pressure when markets open Monday.

articles.headline: U.S.-Israel Strikes Kill Iran's Supreme Leader, Sparking Regional War
articles.subheadline: Ayatollah Khamenei's death in Saturday's opening salvo triggers Iranian missile barrages across the Middle East and global energy turmoil
articles.body:
A massive joint U.S.-Israeli military operation killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on Saturday when strikes flattened his compound in Tehran, igniting the broadest military conflict the Middle East has seen in decades. By Monday, the fighting had drawn in Hezbollah, rattled global energy markets, and left hundreds dead across the region.

The operation, dubbed "Epic Fury" by U.S. Central Command, targeted Iran's missile infrastructure, military installations, and senior leadership. In addition to Khamenei — who had ruled Iran since 1989 — the Israel Defense Forces confirmed the deaths of Defence Council secretary Ali Shamkhani, Defence Minister Brig Gen Aziz Nasirzadeh, and Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps commander Gen Mohammad Pakpour. The IRGC has named Ahmad Vahidi as its new commander-in-chief. Iran's Red Crescent reported 555 people killed across 130 locations as of Monday, including more than 150 — among them children — in a strike on a school near an IRGC base in southern Iran. President Donald Trump said the goal was to prevent Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon, warning Iranian forces to lay down their arms or "face certain death" and urging citizens to overthrow the clerical government.

Iran's response has been swift and far-reaching. The IRGC launched ballistic missiles and drones at Israel, killing nine people in a residential area of Beit Shemesh, while also striking targets in Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Oman, and a British military base in Cyprus. Civilian sites including hotels in Dubai were hit. The U.S. military confirmed four American soldiers killed and five wounded, while three U.S. jets were downed over Kuwait in what appears to have been a friendly fire incident. On Monday, Hezbollah opened a new front by attacking Israel from Lebanon, saying it sought to avenge Khamenei's assassination, prompting Israeli strikes on Beirut and southern Lebanon.

The economic fallout is already severe. Qatar halted liquefied natural gas production after Iranian strikes, triggering a 50 percent spike in gas prices. Iranian drones struck Saudi Arabia's Ras Tanura refinery, causing a partial shutdown, and Iran warned all vessels against transiting the Strait of Hormuz — a chokepoint for roughly 20 percent of global oil and gas shipments. Oil prices surged 10 percent on fears of prolonged disruption.

A transitional leadership council comprising President Masoud Pezeshkian, judiciary chief Gholamhossein Mohseni Ejei, and senior cleric Alireza Arafi is governing Iran while the 88-member Assembly of Experts prepares to select a new supreme leader — a process complicated by ongoing strikes. Trump told CNN on Monday that he envisions the campaign lasting approximately four weeks and that operations are running "a little ahead of schedule." Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the campaign would "continue as long as it is needed." Thousands of flights across the region have been grounded and internet connectivity in Iran has been almost entirely severed, leaving the conflict's trajectory deeply uncertain.

articles.headline: Cyberattacks and Internet Blackout Accompany U.S. Airstrikes on Iran
articles.subheadline: Hackers hijacked a popular prayer app to broadcast anti-regime messages as Iran's internet collapsed to near-zero connectivity
articles.body:
A coordinated digital assault accompanied the U.S. and Israel-led military strikes on Iran that killed Supreme Leader Ali Hosseini Khamenei and senior leadership on Saturday, as cyberattacks disrupted communications infrastructure and hackers commandeered a widely used mobile application to broadcast anti-government messages to millions of Iranians.

As missiles struck targets across Tehran and other Iranian cities early Saturday morning, users of BadeSaba — a popular Islamic prayer app with more than five million downloads — began receiving a barrage of unsolicited notifications. The messages called for a "reckoning" and promised amnesty to anyone who rose up against government forces, according to Wired. One notification declared that the Iranian regime would "pay for their cruel and merciless actions against the innocent people of Iran." The identity of the attackers remains unknown, though The Jerusalem Post reported that cyberattacks were deployed as an integrated component of the U.S.-Israeli military campaign, intended to degrade Iran's ability to mount an effective response.

The digital disruption extended well beyond a single compromised app. Doug Madory, director of internet analysis at network intelligence firm Kentik, reported on Bluesky that Iranian internet connectivity plunged to near-zero levels shortly after the airstrikes began. Cloudflare, the major networking infrastructure company, independently confirmed the collapse of Iran's internet on Saturday. The blackout followed what had already been the country's longest internet shutdown to date, imposed during weeks of mass protests in which thousands were killed — unrest that preceded failed negotiations between Tehran and Washington.

The physical and digital conflict has also generated significant collateral damage across the broader Middle East. Amazon reported an outage at its data center in the United Arab Emirates after Iranian retaliatory missiles struck the coastal nation. The company attributed the disruption to "objects that struck the data center, creating sparks and fire." Critical commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz near Iran has also ground to a halt, threatening vital e-commerce supply chains that depend on the waterway's air and sea routes.

Both the United States and Israel have previously been suspected of conducting cyberattacks targeting Iranian banks and cryptocurrency exchanges as part of a broader pressure campaign against the regime, which has held power since 1989. The integration of cyber operations with conventional military strikes on this scale marks a significant escalation in hybrid warfare tactics, raising questions about the vulnerability of civilian digital infrastructure — from prayer apps to cloud data centers — in modern armed conflicts.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Governance Battle Heats Up as First BIP-110 Block Is Mined
articles.subheadline: Ocean mining pool signals support for restricting non-financial data on Bitcoin, while critics inscribe protest images onchain
articles.body:
The long-simmering debate over what Bitcoin's blockchain should be used for erupted into open conflict this week when mining pool Ocean produced the first block signaling support for BIP-110, a proposal that would temporarily cap the amount of arbitrary, non-monetary data that can be embedded in Bitcoin transactions.

BIP-110, which evolved from earlier draft proposals, would reimpose strict limits on transaction output sizes and arbitrary data fields for approximately one year. Proponents argue that large inscriptions, images, and so-called OP_RETURN payloads — script instructions that allow users to permanently embed arbitrary data like text or images into the blockchain — have effectively turned Bitcoin into a bulletin board, crowding out its core function as sound monetary infrastructure and imposing storage burdens on the node operators who keep the network running.

The opposition has been swift and pointed. Blockstream CEO Adam Back warned that attempting to enforce consensus-level restrictions on transaction content could undermine Bitcoin's credibility as a neutral, permissionless network. Back questioned whether BIP-110 commands sufficient support among miners and node operators, cautioning that pushing forward without broad consensus risks splitting the blockchain — one of the most feared outcomes in cryptocurrency governance. In a vivid act of protest, a developer inscribed a 66-kilobyte image into a single Bitcoin transaction, demonstrating that large data payloads can be encoded on the blockchain even without relying on OP_RETURN, effectively challenging the proposal's practical enforceability.

The dispute cuts to a philosophical fault line that has defined Bitcoin politics for over a decade: whether the network should aggressively defend a narrow monetary mission or maintain maximal neutrality toward how participants use block space. The Ordinals movement, which enabled NFT-like inscriptions on Bitcoin beginning in 2023, reignited this tension by driving a surge in non-financial data usage. BIP-110 represents the most formalized attempt yet to roll back that trend through a soft fork.

With Bitcoin trading near $69,200 amid broader geopolitical volatility, the governance fight adds another layer of uncertainty for market participants. The signaling process is still in its earliest stages — a single block from one mining pool is far from the threshold needed to activate a soft fork — but the battle lines are now clearly drawn. How the community resolves this question will shape not just Bitcoin's technical architecture but its identity as a decentralized system resistant to top-down control.

articles.headline: European Gas Prices Spike Over 50% After Iran Drone Strike Shuts Qatar LNG Facility
articles.subheadline: Closure of world's largest LNG export plant sends shockwaves through global energy markets amid escalating Middle East tensions
articles.body:
European natural gas prices surged as much as 54% on Monday after Qatar halted production at the world's largest liquefied natural gas export facility following an Iranian drone attack, triggering one of the sharpest single-day moves in the global energy market in recent years.

The shutdown of Qatar's flagship LNG complex — which supplies a significant share of the world's seaborne natural gas trade — immediately reverberated across energy markets, with traders scrambling to assess the duration of the outage and its cascading effects on supply chains stretching from Asia to Europe. Qatar is one of the world's top three LNG exporters alongside the United States and Australia, and any sustained disruption to its output has the potential to reshape global gas flows for weeks or months.

The attack marks a dramatic escalation in regional hostilities involving Iran and raises urgent questions about the vulnerability of critical energy infrastructure in the Persian Gulf. Qatar's LNG facilities have long been considered among the most strategically important energy assets on the planet, underpinning long-term supply contracts with buyers across Europe and Asia. European nations, many of which pivoted aggressively toward LNG imports after cutting ties with Russian pipeline gas following the invasion of Ukraine, are particularly exposed to any prolonged disruption.

The scale of the price reaction — with European benchmark gas rallying more than 30% even after pulling back from intraday highs — underscores just how tight global LNG markets remain heading into the second quarter of 2026. Inventories across European storage sites had already been drawing down faster than seasonal norms, and the loss of Qatari supply could accelerate that trend, raising the specter of renewed energy security concerns ahead of next winter.

Qatari authorities had not yet provided a timeline for restoring production as of Monday afternoon. Analysts warned that the geopolitical risk premium embedded in gas prices is unlikely to dissipate quickly, even if physical damage to the facility proves limited. The incident is expected to dominate discussions among energy ministers and could prompt renewed calls for diversification of LNG supply sources and accelerated investment in strategic gas reserves across importing nations.

articles.headline: Neural Network-Enhanced Kalman Filter Tames Shock Waves in Fluid Simulations
articles.subheadline: Researchers combine deep learning with classical data assimilation to eliminate spurious oscillations near shocks in compressible flows
articles.body:
A team of seven researchers has introduced a fundamentally new approach to one of computational fluid dynamics' most stubborn problems: accurately assimilating observational data into simulations of compressible flows containing shock waves. The method, called the neural ensemble Kalman filter (neural EnKF), embeds deep neural networks within a classical statistical framework to avoid the nonphysical artifacts that have long plagued standard techniques.

The work, led by Xu-Hui Zhou and collaborators including Lorenzo Beronilla, Michael K. Sleeman, Hangchuan Hu, Matthias Morzfeld, Andrew M. Stuart, and Tamer A. Zaki, was posted to the arXiv preprint server on February 26. The paper identifies a precise mathematical reason why the standard ensemble Kalman filter fails near shocks: uncertain shock locations produce bimodal forecast distributions that violate the near-Gaussian assumptions on which the filter depends. This violation generates spurious oscillations and nonphysical features in the resulting predictions — a well-known headache for engineers and scientists modeling everything from supersonic aircraft to astrophysical explosions.

The neural EnKF addresses this by transforming the problem into a different mathematical space. Rather than performing data assimilation directly on the flow variables, the method maps the forecast ensemble of shocked flows onto the weights and biases of a deep neural network, then carries out the Kalman update in that parameter space. The nonlinear mapping effectively encodes both sharp discontinuities and smooth flow features in a representation where updates remain well-behaved. A critical ingredient is what the authors call physics-informed transfer learning, which ensures that the neural network parameters vary smoothly across the ensemble — a necessary condition for the approach to work correctly.

The team validated the neural EnKF through a systematic series of numerical experiments of increasing complexity: the inviscid Burgers' equation, a standard one-dimensional test case; Sod's shock tube problem, a canonical benchmark in compressible gas dynamics; and a two-dimensional blast wave simulation. In each case, the method successfully avoided the spurious oscillations that undermined the standard EnKF.

The implications extend across multiple domains where data assimilation meets compressible flow physics, including aerospace engineering, weather prediction involving atmospheric shock phenomena, and astrophysical modeling. By resolving a fundamental statistical incompatibility rather than applying ad hoc fixes, the neural EnKF could offer a more principled path forward for real-time prediction systems that must reconcile sparse observations with complex shock-dominated simulations.

articles.headline: Iranian Strikes Shut Down Major Gulf Energy Infrastructure
articles.subheadline: Strait of Hormuz shipping halted as Qatar's largest LNG facility and Saudi Arabia's top refinery forced offline
articles.body:
Iranian military strikes have caused severe disruption to critical energy infrastructure across the Persian Gulf, effectively halting shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and forcing the closure of two of the world's most important hydrocarbon facilities, according to a Bloomberg report published on March 2.

The attacks shut down Qatar's Ras Laffan complex — the largest liquefied natural gas export facility on Earth — as well as Saudi Arabia's biggest oil refinery. The Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption passes, saw traffic grind to a near-complete stop as Iranian projectiles criss-crossed the vital shipping lane. The combined disruptions represent one of the most consequential strikes against global energy supply chains in decades.

The scale of the infrastructure damage threatens to send shockwaves through global energy markets. Qatar is the world's leading LNG exporter, and the Ras Laffan facility is the linchpin of that trade, supplying major economies across Asia and Europe. Saudi Arabia's refining capacity, meanwhile, is central to global fuel supply. The simultaneous targeting of both assets, alongside the effective blockade of Hormuz, creates a compounding supply crisis that energy markets have long treated as a worst-case scenario.

For years, defense analysts and energy strategists have war-gamed the possibility of Iran leveraging its geographic position along the strait to choke global oil and gas flows. That theoretical risk has now materialized. Nations heavily dependent on Gulf energy imports — particularly in East Asia and parts of Europe still reliant on spot LNG cargoes — face immediate supply uncertainty. Strategic petroleum reserves held by the United States, Japan, South Korea, and members of the International Energy Agency may come under pressure for release.

The full extent of physical damage to the Qatari and Saudi facilities remains unclear, as does the timeline for any resumption of operations or safe passage through the strait. Markets are expected to react sharply when trading opens, with crude oil and LNG futures likely to surge. The attacks mark a dramatic escalation in the ongoing conflict with Iran and raise urgent questions about the vulnerability of the world's most concentrated energy corridor.

articles.headline: Iranian Crypto Outflows Surge 700% Minutes After U.S.-Israeli Strikes on Tehran
articles.subheadline: Blockchain analytics firm Elliptic traces rapid capital flight from Iran's largest exchange to overseas platforms
articles.body:
Cryptocurrency transactions flowing out of Iran's largest digital asset exchange spiked 700% within minutes of the first U.S.-Israeli airstrikes on Tehran over the weekend, according to blockchain analytics firm Elliptic, in what researchers describe as a dramatic example of crypto-enabled capital flight during a geopolitical crisis.

Elliptic's analysis, published Monday, found that outgoing transaction volumes from Nobitex — an exchange with more than 11 million users that processed $7.2 billion in crypto trades in 2025 — surged almost immediately after the coordinated strikes began. Initial blockchain tracing showed funds moving to overseas exchanges that have historically received significant inflows from Iranian users. "This potentially represents capital flight from Iran that bypasses the traditional banking system," said Dr. Tom Robinson, Elliptic's co-founder and chief scientist. The weekend strikes killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and marked a severe escalation of the broader Middle East conflict, rattling global markets and sending crude oil prices sharply higher.

The outflow pattern is not unprecedented. Robinson flagged additional surges in Iranian crypto activity earlier in 2026, including the largest spike on January 9 following widespread anti-regime demonstrations and a government-imposed internet blackout. Two further surges coincided with new U.S. sanctions announcements targeting Iranian actors, suggesting that cryptocurrency has become a systematic tool for mitigating the impact of economic restrictions. Elliptic has previously linked Nobitex to financial activity aligned with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and reported that Iran's central bank appeared to use the platform in January to support the weakening rial.

The airstrikes also jolted crypto markets broadly. Bitcoin fell below $64,000 before recovering to the mid-$60,000 range, trading around $65,500 at the time of Elliptic's report — down roughly 2%. Ether dropped 3.8% to approximately $1,930. The sell-off underscored digital assets' growing sensitivity to geopolitical shocks, even as several major tokens rebounded above pre-strike levels relatively quickly.

The findings spotlight the expanding role of cryptocurrencies in Iran's estimated $7.8 billion shadow economy. Nobitex allows users to convert Iranian rials into crypto and withdraw to external wallets, effectively offering a pathway around banking controls that have constrained the country under decades of international sanctions. U.S. authorities have been investigating whether digital asset platforms have enabled state-linked actors to access hard currency outside the traditional financial system — a probe that the weekend's dramatic outflow data is likely to intensify.

articles.headline: Rice University AI Maps Hidden Chemical Upheaval Across Alzheimer's Brain
articles.subheadline: First dye-free molecular atlas reveals widespread metabolic disruption far beyond amyloid plaques in landmark study
articles.body:
A team at Rice University has produced the first comprehensive, dye-free molecular atlas of an Alzheimer's-affected brain, revealing that the disease involves sweeping chemical disruptions that extend far beyond the amyloid plaques long considered its hallmark. The findings, published in ACS Applied Materials and Interfaces, suggest Alzheimer's may be better understood as a whole-brain metabolic disorder rather than a localized protein-aggregation problem.

The researchers combined hyperspectral Raman imaging — a laser-based technique that detects the unique chemical fingerprints of molecules within tissue — with machine learning to scan entire brain slices from both healthy and Alzheimer's-affected animal models. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on dyes or fluorescent tags, the method captured the brain's chemical landscape in its unaltered state, generating thousands of overlapping measurements per slice. "This means we observed the brain as is, capturing a complete, unaltered portrait of its chemical makeup," said Ziyang Wang, a doctoral student in electrical and computer engineering at Rice and a first author of the study.

The enormous datasets were analyzed using both unsupervised and supervised machine learning algorithms. Unsupervised models identified natural chemical patterns without prior assumptions, while supervised models were trained to distinguish Alzheimer's tissue from healthy samples. The results showed that Alzheimer's-related chemical changes are distributed unevenly across the brain — some regions exhibit dramatic molecular shifts while others remain relatively spared. This asymmetry may help explain the gradual onset of symptoms and the limited success of treatments that target a single mechanism.

Among the most striking findings were significant variations in cholesterol and glycogen levels, particularly in the hippocampus and cortex — regions critical to memory formation. Cholesterol plays an essential role in maintaining brain cell structure, while glycogen serves as a local energy reserve. "Together, these findings support the idea that Alzheimer's involves broader disruptions in brain structure and energy balance, not only protein buildup and misfolding," said Shengxi Huang, associate professor at Rice and the study's corresponding author.

The research, funded by the National Science Foundation, the National Institutes of Health, and the Welch Foundation, adds to a growing body of evidence challenging the amyloid-centric model that has dominated Alzheimer's research — and drug development — for decades. With Alzheimer's claiming more lives annually than breast and prostate cancer combined, the team hopes its molecular mapping approach will eventually support earlier diagnosis and inform therapeutic strategies that address the disease's full metabolic complexity rather than a single pathological feature.

articles.headline: Turkey's Central Bank Moves to Defend Lira Amid Iran Crisis Selloff
articles.subheadline: Turkish stocks plunge more than 5% as escalating regional tensions ripple through emerging markets
articles.body:
Turkey's central bank stepped in to support the lira on Monday as escalating tensions involving Iran sent shockwaves through regional financial markets, triggering a sharp selloff in Turkish equities and putting pressure on the country's currency.

Turkish stocks fell more than 5% in the session, marking one of the steepest single-day declines in recent months, as investors fled risk assets tied to the Middle East and broader emerging markets. The selloff reflected growing fears that the Iran crisis could destabilize an already fragile regional economic landscape, with Turkey — situated at the geographic crossroads of Europe and the Middle East — bearing an outsized share of market anxiety.

The central bank's intervention to prop up the lira underscores the precarious position Ankara finds itself in. Turkey has spent years working to rebuild credibility with international investors after a series of unconventional monetary policy decisions eroded confidence in its institutions. A sustained period of lira weakness could reignite inflationary pressures that policymakers have fought to contain, potentially forcing the central bank into difficult choices between defending the currency and maintaining economic growth.

The broader regional selloff highlights how geopolitical risk continues to serve as a dominant force in emerging market pricing. Investors have long treated Turkey as a bellwether for Middle Eastern stability, and the speed of Monday's moves suggests positioning had grown complacent in the face of simmering tensions. Energy markets are also in focus, as any disruption to Iranian oil supply or transit routes through the region could amplify inflationary headwinds globally.

Analysts will be watching closely in the coming sessions to gauge whether the central bank's lira defense proves sufficient or whether more aggressive measures — including potential rate adjustments or capital flow management — become necessary. For now, the episode serves as a stark reminder that Turkey's financial markets remain acutely sensitive to geopolitical developments on its doorstep, and that the hard-won stability of recent months can unravel quickly when regional conflicts escalate.

articles.headline: Massive Collaboration Maps Synthetic Data Use in MR Spectroscopy
articles.subheadline: A 78-author working group under the ISMRM reviews how artificial datasets are reshaping brain chemistry research
articles.body:
A sweeping review by more than 78 researchers across dozens of institutions has catalogued the growing role of synthetic data in Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy, a medical imaging technique used to measure chemical concentrations in living tissue. The paper, published on the arXiv preprint server in February 2026, represents one of the largest collaborative efforts to date in standardizing practices around artificially generated datasets in the MRS field.

The review was conducted by the MRS Synthetic Data Working Group, operating under the Code and Data Sharing Committee of the MRS Study Group within the International Society for Magnetic Resonance in Medicine (ISMRM). Led by John T. LaMaster, Aaron T. Gudmundson, and senior author Antonia Kaiser, the team drew on both published literature and in-house methodologies developed across participating laboratories to assess how synthetic data is currently generated, validated, and applied.

Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy enables non-invasive measurement of metabolite concentrations in the brain and other organs, providing critical diagnostic information for neurological and metabolic disorders. However, the technique has long been constrained by limited availability of training data — particularly from clinical populations with rare conditions — making it difficult to develop and validate robust analysis pipelines. Synthetic data, the authors argue, has emerged as an essential tool for addressing these gaps, offering controlled environments to optimize signal acquisition, validate processing software, train deep learning models, and investigate sources of unexplained variance in patient scans.

The 20,493-word manuscript, accompanied by six figures and supplementary tables, covers the full landscape of synthetic data generation techniques in MRS, from physics-based signal simulations to more recent machine learning approaches. The authors highlight applications including benchmarking of spectral fitting algorithms, augmentation of limited clinical datasets for neural network training, and systematic investigation of measurement uncertainties that are difficult to isolate in real patient data.

The collaboration underscores a broader trend across medical imaging: as deep learning methods become standard analytical tools, the demand for large, well-characterized training datasets has outpaced what clinical studies alone can provide. By establishing community-wide best practices for synthetic data generation and reporting, the working group aims to improve reproducibility — a persistent challenge in MRS research — and lower barriers for smaller laboratories seeking to adopt advanced computational methods. The paper is currently available as a preprint and has not yet undergone peer review.

articles.headline: OpenAI Defends Pentagon Deal as Altman Admits It Was 'Rushed'
articles.subheadline: The AI company outlines red lines on surveillance and autonomous weapons after stepping in when Anthropic's negotiations collapsed
articles.body:
OpenAI has published new details about its agreement to deploy AI models in classified Pentagon environments, a deal CEO Sam Altman conceded was "definitely rushed" and has generated substantial backlash — including a surge of users to rival Anthropic's products.

The arrangement came together rapidly after negotiations between Anthropic and the Department of Defense broke down on Friday. President Donald Trump subsequently directed federal agencies to phase out Anthropic's technology over six months, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated the company a supply-chain risk. OpenAI stepped in with its own agreement, prompting immediate questions about whether it had compromised on safety commitments that Anthropic had refused to bend on.

In a blog post published over the weekend, OpenAI outlined three explicit prohibitions in its deal: the models cannot be used for mass domestic surveillance, autonomous weapon systems, or high-stakes automated decisions such as social credit-style scoring. The company emphasized what it called a "multi-layered approach" to enforcement, noting that it retains full control over its safety stack, deploys exclusively via cloud infrastructure, keeps cleared OpenAI personnel in the loop, and maintains contractual protections. Katrina Mulligan, OpenAI's head of national security partnerships, argued on LinkedIn that the cloud API-only deployment architecture physically prevents models from being integrated into weapons systems, sensors, or operational hardware — a safeguard she said matters more than contract language alone.

Critics were quick to scrutinize the fine print. Techdirt's Mike Masnick pointed out that the agreement's reference to Executive Order 12333 as a governing framework for private data collection could effectively permit certain forms of domestic surveillance. Masnick described the executive order as the legal mechanism the NSA has historically used to capture communications involving U.S. persons by tapping lines outside the country. The backlash was not limited to policy analysts: Anthropic's Claude chatbot overtook ChatGPT in Apple's App Store rankings on Saturday, a concrete measure of consumer sentiment.

Altman addressed the criticism directly on X, framing the deal as an attempt to de-escalate tensions between the administration and the broader AI industry. "If we are right and this does lead to a de-escalation between the DoW and the industry, we will look like geniuses," he wrote. "If not, we will continue to be characterized as rushed and uncareful." OpenAI also noted in its blog post that it does not know why Anthropic was unable to reach a similar agreement, adding that it hopes other labs will consider the framework. The episode has crystallized a widening strategic divide in the AI industry over how far companies should go in accommodating government demands — and what safeguards are sufficient when the client is the world's most powerful military.

articles.headline: Polymarket Handled $529M in Wagers Tied to U.S.-Israeli Strikes on Iran
articles.subheadline: Analytics firm flags six accounts that netted $1 million in suspected insider trading on conflict timing
articles.body:
Prediction market platform Polymarket processed $529 million in trading volume on contracts linked to the U.S. and Israeli military strikes on Iran, raising urgent questions about whether anonymous betting markets are enabling profiteering from armed conflict and potentially incentivizing leaks of classified information.

The staggering figure, first reported by Bloomberg, reflects a surge of speculative activity around the timing and likelihood of the attack. More troublingly, blockchain analytics firm Bubblemaps SA identified six newly created accounts that collectively earned $1 million by correctly wagering that the United States would strike Iran by February 28. The pattern of freshly opened accounts placing large, precisely timed bets is a classic indicator of insider trading, according to Bubblemaps CEO Nicolas Vaiman. "The circulation of information involving war or conflict," combined with Polymarket's pseudonymous structure, "can create incentives for informed participants to act early," Vaiman said.

The Iran-related contracts were not the only troubling signal. As far back as January, analytics firm Polysights flagged a suspicious spike in bets on whether Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei — who has since been killed — would leave his post by the end of March. That market effectively placed a financial bounty on a world leader's death, a scenario prediction market operators have long struggled to address. Kalshi CEO Tarek Mansour sought to distance his platform from such concerns, stating that Kalshi does not "list markets directly tied to death" and that when potential outcomes involve death, the company designs rules "to prevent people from profiting from death." Mansour added that Kalshi would reimburse all fees from Iran-related bets.

The episode crystallizes a dilemma that has shadowed prediction markets since their rise into the mainstream: the same features that make them powerful information-aggregation tools — anonymity, liquidity, and real-money stakes — also make them potential conduits for intelligence leaks and morally hazardous speculation. With half a billion dollars flowing through contracts pegged to a military operation, regulators and national security officials may face mounting pressure to scrutinize how these platforms handle geopolitically sensitive markets.

Neither Polymarket nor U.S. regulatory agencies have publicly commented on whether investigations into the suspicious trading activity are underway. The incident is likely to intensify the ongoing debate in Washington over how — and whether — prediction markets should be regulated when their contracts intersect with matters of war, espionage, and human life.

articles.headline: Strategy Adds 3,015 Bitcoin for $204M, Holdings Now Top 720,000 BTC
articles.subheadline: Michael Saylor's firm continues accumulating despite sitting on billions in unrealized losses as bitcoin trades near $66,000
articles.body:
Michael Saylor's Strategy added another 3,015 bitcoin to its already massive corporate treasury last week, spending approximately $204.1 million at an average price of $67,700 per coin, according to a filing published Monday.

The purchase brings Strategy's total holdings to 720,737 BTC, acquired for a cumulative $54.77 billion at an average cost basis of roughly $75,985 per coin. With bitcoin trading around $66,000 on Monday morning — well below that average — the company is sitting on a substantial unrealized loss across its aggregate position. At current prices, Strategy's stack is valued at approximately $47.6 billion, implying a paper loss exceeding $7 billion.

To finance the latest acquisition, the company raised approximately $229.9 million through common stock sales and an additional $7.1 million from its Variable Rate Series A Perpetual Stretch Preferred Stock, known as STRC. The preferred stock instrument has become an increasingly important funding mechanism for Strategy, which recently boosted the STRC dividend to 11.5 percent as its common shares, trading under the ticker MSTR, extended a monthly losing streak to eight consecutive months.

The purchase comes amid a turbulent period for both crypto and traditional markets. An escalating U.S.-Iran conflict has injected significant volatility across asset classes, with bitcoin actually outperforming equities in recent risk-off sessions. Some analysts have pointed to a potential "debasement trade" narrative — the idea that geopolitical instability and wartime fiscal expansion could ultimately benefit hard assets like bitcoin — though the cryptocurrency remains sharply below highs reached earlier in the cycle.

Strategy remains by far the largest publicly traded corporate holder of bitcoin, a distinction Saylor has pursued relentlessly since the company began its accumulation strategy in 2020. The company's approach of continuously issuing equity and preferred stock to fund bitcoin purchases has drawn both admirers and critics on Wall Street. MSTR shares traded flat in early Monday action, reflecting investor uncertainty about whether the firm's leveraged bet on bitcoin will ultimately pay off at current price levels.

articles.headline: Oil Surges 10% as Iran Threatens to Close Strait of Hormuz
articles.subheadline: Ship attacks near critical waterway send energy prices spiking and European equities tumbling amid widening Middle East conflict
articles.body:
Global oil prices surged as much as 10% on Monday after Iran launched strikes across the Middle East and warned vessels away from the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supply transits daily. The escalation triggered broad sell-offs across European equity markets and raised fresh concerns about a potential inflationary shock to the global economy.

Brent crude briefly touched $82 per barrel before settling back to around $79, while US-traded oil climbed approximately 7.6% to $72.20. Natural gas prices spiked as much as 25%. The moves came after at least three ships were attacked near the strait over the weekend, with the UK Maritime Trade Operations Centre confirming two vessels were struck and a projectile detonated in close proximity to a third. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps claimed three British and American tankers had been hit by missiles.

Shipping through the strait has ground to a near halt. According to ship-tracking platform Kpler, at least 150 tankers have dropped anchor in open Gulf waters beyond the chokepoint, with only a handful of Iranian and Chinese vessels passing through. "Because of Iran's threats, the strait is effectively closed," Kpler analyst Homayoun Falakshahi told the BBC, noting that insurance costs for transit have skyrocketed. Danish shipping giant Maersk announced it would pause sailings through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait and the Suez Canal, rerouting vessels around the Cape of Good Hope.

European stock markets took a significant hit. Germany's DAX fell 2.1%, France's CAC-40 dropped 1.8%, and London's FTSE 100 declined 1%. Airlines, including British Airways parent IAG, were among the hardest hit as Middle East airspace disruptions forced thousands of flight cancellations. Banks including Barclays, Standard Chartered, and HSBC also slid on fears that sustained energy price increases could reignite inflation and delay anticipated interest rate cuts. Gold, the traditional safe-haven asset, climbed 2% to $5,388 per ounce.

Analysts offered a range of outlooks. Saul Kavonic of MST Marquee said the market was not yet panicking, noting that oil production and transport infrastructure has not been directly targeted. But others warned that a prolonged closure of the strait could push crude above $100 per barrel. Subitha Subramaniam, chief economist at Sarasin & Partners, cautioned that persistently elevated oil prices would "cascade into other prices such as food, agriculture, industrial commodities" and bleed into broader inflation, potentially forcing the Bank of England to hold interest rates at 3.75% despite recent signals of further cuts. The OPEC+ group moved Sunday to increase output by 206,000 barrels per day, though experts expressed doubt the measure would meaningfully offset disruptions of this magnitude.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Drops Below $66,000 as Iran Strikes Saudi Oil Infrastructure
articles.subheadline: Escalating Middle East conflict sends S&P 500 futures down 1.4% while oil surges 7%, testing crypto's safe-haven narrative
articles.body:
Bitcoin retreated sharply below $66,000 on Sunday after Iran reportedly struck a major Saudi Arabian oil refinery, escalating a Middle East conflict that has rattled global financial markets and sent crude prices surging.

The leading cryptocurrency fell to approximately $65,881 after briefly touching $67,000 during early Asian trading hours on March 2, erasing its session gains as geopolitical risk intensified. S&P 500 e-mini futures dropped 1.4% to 6,790, reversing an earlier climb to 6,857, while oil prices on both Brent and WTI benchmarks jumped more than 7%. The selloff followed reports from open-source intelligence accounts that Iran had launched missile attacks on U.S. assets in Bahrain, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates, and had struck Saudi Aramco's Ras Tanura refinery — a facility operated by the world's largest oil producer.

The conflict erupted Saturday after the United States and Israel launched what has been characterized as a pre-emptive strike aimed at degrading Iran's missile capabilities and nuclear ambitions. Iran's response has been to broaden the theater of operations, targeting energy infrastructure and military assets across the Persian Gulf region. The BBC reported that Israel simultaneously carried out a fresh round of airstrikes in Lebanon against Hezbollah, Iran's most prominent regional proxy.

Stephen Coltman, head of macro at crypto asset manager 21Shares, said Iran's strategy is designed to raise the economic cost of the conflict for Washington. "Iran's strategy to date has been to raise the cost to the United States of sustaining the conflict by launching attacks on neighboring countries and attempting to disrupt the flow of oil and LNG through the Strait of Hormuz," Coltman told CoinDesk. He noted that wars are "generally inflationary, driving up commodity prices and widening fiscal deficits," suggesting that perceived store-of-value assets like bitcoin could eventually benefit from a broader debasement trade — even if the initial market reaction has been risk-off across the board.

So far, however, bitcoin has shown little evidence of safe-haven demand, falling in lockstep with equities rather than rallying alongside gold and oil. The correlation underscores a persistent tension in crypto markets: while proponents argue bitcoin should function as digital gold during geopolitical crises, its behavior in the opening days of the Iran conflict has more closely resembled that of a risk asset. With the situation still rapidly evolving and energy markets under acute pressure, traders appear to be reducing exposure broadly rather than rotating into alternative stores of value.

articles.headline: Qatar Halts LNG Output at Ras Laffan After Military Strike
articles.subheadline: QatarEnergy shuts down world's largest liquefied natural gas facility following attack on critical infrastructure
articles.body:
QatarEnergy has suspended liquefied natural gas production at its Ras Laffan Industrial City complex following military attacks on the facility, the state-owned energy giant confirmed in a statement. The shutdown marks one of the most significant disruptions to global energy supply infrastructure in recent memory, given Ras Laffan's role as the single largest source of LNG on the planet.

The Ras Laffan complex, located on Qatar's northeastern coast roughly 80 kilometers from Doha, is the centerpiece of the country's vast natural gas operations. Qatar is one of the world's top three LNG exporters alongside the United States and Australia, and Ras Laffan serves as the processing and export hub for production from the massive North Field — the largest natural gas reservoir ever discovered. The facility's output supplies major economies across Asia and Europe, making any sustained interruption a potential flashpoint for global energy markets.

Details surrounding the nature of the military attack remain limited. QatarEnergy did not immediately disclose the extent of damage to infrastructure, the timeline for potential resumption of operations, or whether there were casualties. The company's terse statement left markets and analysts scrambling to assess the severity of the disruption and its downstream consequences for LNG supply contracts and spot pricing.

The timing of the attack heightens its potential economic impact. Qatar had been in the midst of a historic expansion of its LNG capacity through the North Field Expansion project, which aimed to boost the country's output from roughly 77 million tonnes per annum to 126 million tonnes by the end of the decade. European nations, which pivoted sharply toward LNG imports after curtailing Russian pipeline gas following Moscow's invasion of Ukraine, have grown increasingly dependent on Qatari supplies to meet heating and industrial demand.

Energy analysts expect natural gas futures to surge when markets fully digest the news, with ripple effects likely across crude oil benchmarks and broader commodity indices. The attack raises urgent questions about the vulnerability of concentrated energy infrastructure in the Persian Gulf region, where a significant share of global hydrocarbon supply passes through a narrow geographic corridor.
